title: Introduction to Information Retrieval
body: Information retrieval is the activity of obtaining information system resources that are relevant to an information need from a collection of those resources. Searches can be based on full-text or other content-based indexing. The field has evolved from simple keyword matching to sophisticated neural approaches.

title: BM25: A Probabilistic Retrieval Model
body: BM25 is a bag-of-words retrieval function that ranks a set of documents based on the query terms appearing in each document, regardless of their proximity within the document. It is a family of scoring functions with slightly different components and parameters. BM25 considers term frequency saturation and document length normalization.

title: Dense Retrieval with Bi-Encoders
body: Dense retrieval methods encode queries and documents into dense vector representations using neural networks such as BERT. The similarity between a query and document is computed as the dot product or cosine similarity of their respective embeddings. This approach captures semantic meaning beyond exact lexical matching.

title: Hybrid Search: Combining Sparse and Dense
body: Hybrid search systems combine the strengths of sparse retrieval like BM25 with dense semantic retrieval. Reciprocal rank fusion is a popular method that merges ranked lists from multiple retrieval systems by summing the reciprocal of each document's rank across systems.

title: Learning to Rank with LambdaMART
body: LambdaMART combines gradient boosted decision trees with the LambdaRank framework to directly optimize information retrieval metrics like NDCG. It computes lambda gradients from pairwise document comparisons, weighting each pair by the change in NDCG that would result from swapping their positions.

title: Neural Cross-Encoder Reranking
body: Cross-encoder models process query-document pairs jointly through a transformer architecture to produce fine-grained relevance scores. Unlike bi-encoders that encode queries and documents independently, cross-encoders capture rich interactions between query and document tokens.

title: Inverted Index Fundamentals
body: An inverted index is a data structure that maps content such as words or numbers to its locations in a set of documents. It is the most popular data structure used in document retrieval systems, enabling efficient full-text searches. Each term points to a posting list containing document IDs and term frequencies.

title: FAISS: Efficient Similarity Search
body: FAISS is a library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors. It contains algorithms that search in sets of vectors of any size, up to ones that possibly do not fit in RAM. FAISS supports GPU acceleration and various index types including flat, IVF, and HNSW.

title: Query Understanding for Search
body: Query understanding encompasses techniques for interpreting user search intent, including intent classification, entity recognition, and query reformulation. Understanding whether a query is navigational, informational, or transactional helps the search engine select the appropriate retrieval strategy.

title: Search Evaluation Metrics
body: Standard evaluation metrics for information retrieval include NDCG (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain), MAP (Mean Average Precision), and MRR (Mean Reciprocal Rank). These metrics measure different aspects of ranking quality and are essential for comparing search system performance.
